statement: Webhook vs polling is not a performance trade-off but an architectural philosophy choice with profound implications for reliability and operational complexity. Polling means your system initiates requests on a schedule — it is simple to implement, easy to debug, and requires no public endpoint, but it introduces latency (up to 1 polling interval per message), wastes API quota on empty polls, and does not scale as the number of monitored conversations grows. Webhooks mean the platform initiates requests to your system when events occur — latency is sub-second, quota is consumed only on real events, but your system must be publicly accessible with a stable HTTPS endpoint, must validate webhook signatures (or a malicious actor can forge events), and must be designed for idempotency (platforms commonly deliver the same webhook twice on network errors). The canonical decision rule: use webhooks in production for any system where message latency matters or where you process more than 100 messages/day per account; use polling for development, for platforms that do not support webhooks (legacy IMAP, some enterprise systems), or for integrations that are genuinely batch-oriented (daily digest). Never mix both for the same channel without a deduplication layer — running both will process every message twice.

Conversation state management is the hardest problem in multi-platform messaging integration, not API authentication or message formatting. Without explicit state management, a bot that works correctly for single-turn interactions will fail on any multi-turn flow (wizard forms, confirmations, onboarding sequences) because the stateless nature of webhooks means each incoming message arrives with no memory of previous exchanges unless the system maintains that memory. The WHY this is hard: conversation state must survive bot restarts, platform API outages, and parallel conversations with thousands of users simultaneously. The correct architecture is explicit state storage (Redis or a database) keyed by (platform, user_id) with a state machine model: each state defines the valid next states and the actions triggered on each transition. An LLM-based bot that reconstructs conversation context from the message history on every turn (context-window approach) is an alternative that works well for open-ended conversations but is expensive and introduces latency — use it for free-form intent handling, but use a state machine for structured flows. Never store conversation state in memory only — a single process restart loses all in-progress conversations without any notification to users.

Do not build a multi-platform messaging integration without a unified message abstraction layer, because each platform's native message object has different schemas and the platform-specific code will spread throughout your entire codebase. Telegram uses chat_id + message_id; Slack uses channel + ts (timestamp as ID); Discord uses channel_id + message_id; WeChat Work uses from_username + msg_id. Without abstraction, a change to Telegram's API version requires touching every piece of business logic that handles Telegram messages — not just the Telegram adapter. The correct architecture defines a canonical message object {platform: str, conversation_id: str, message_id: str, sender_id: str, sender_name: str, text: str | None, media: list[MediaAttachment], timestamp: datetime, reply_to_id: str | None, raw: dict} and platform adapters that translate to/from this canonical form. All business logic operates on canonical messages; only adapters know about platform-specific schemas. This also makes adding a new platform a bounded change (one new adapter) rather than a cross-cutting change. The raw field preserves the original platform payload for debugging and for accessing platform-specific features not captured by the canonical schema.

Telegram Bot API rate limiting is enforced at two levels that must be handled independently: per-bot global limits (30 messages/second maximum; in practice, bursting above 20/second triggers 429 errors) and per-chat limits (1 message/second per individual chat, 20 messages/minute to the same group). An automation that sends a notification blast to 1,000 users will hit the per-chat limit on the first user if it sends more than 1 message per second to the same chat, and will hit the global limit if it sends messages to different users faster than 30/second. The correct implementation uses a rate-limited queue (token bucket algorithm) with separate buckets for global rate and per-chat rate. When a 429 Too Many Requests response arrives, parse the retry_after field from the response body (Telegram always includes it) and pause that specific chat's queue for exactly that duration rather than applying a fixed backoff. For broadcast scenarios, batch messages across different chats to maximize throughput while staying under per-chat limits: send one message to chat A, one to chat B, one to chat C (rotating) rather than 3 messages to chat A then 3 to chat B.

Discord bot implementation must handle the Gateway intents system introduced in Discord API v8+ or the bot will silently receive no messages. Gateway intents are explicit permission flags that the bot declares at connection time, specifying which event types it wants to receive. The critical intents for a message-handling bot are MESSAGE_CONTENT (privileged — requires explicit enable in the Discord Developer Portal AND in the bot code) and GUILD_MESSAGES. Without MESSAGE_CONTENT intent enabled, the message.content field is empty string for all messages from users that are not @mentioning the bot or sending DMs — there is no error, the field is simply empty, which causes silent processing failures. Additionally, Discord enforces a rate limit of 50 requests per second globally per bot token, with a separate 5 requests per 5 seconds per channel for message sending. For bots serving many guilds (servers), implement a global request queue with per-route bucket tracking using the X-RateLimit-Bucket, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset-After response headers that Discord includes on every API response.

Slack bot architecture must choose between Bolt framework and raw API calls based on deployment complexity, not preference. Bolt (Python, JavaScript, Java) handles Socket Mode, OAuth flow, middleware, and retry logic out of the box — for production bots, it eliminates approximately 2,000 lines of boilerplate. The critical Slack-specific behavior to understand: Slack's Events API requires a 200 OK response within 3 seconds of receiving a webhook or it will retry the event (up to 3 retries with exponential backoff). For event handlers that trigger LLM calls (which take 2–10 seconds), the pattern is: (1) immediately return 200 OK to Slack; (2) enqueue the event in a background task queue (Celery, RQ, or asyncio queue); (3) process asynchronously and post the response via chat.postMessage. Failing to respond within 3 seconds causes duplicate processing — a 5-second LLM call will be retried 3 times, processing the same message 4 times and sending 4 responses to the user. The Bolt framework handles this automatically with its acknowledge() pattern; with raw API calls, you must implement the async response pattern manually.

Do not implement WeChat Work (企业微信/WeCom) bot integration assuming it works like Telegram or Slack, because WeChat Work's API has fundamentally different authentication, message delivery guarantees, and content restrictions. The authentication flow uses a corp_id + corpsecret pair that generates a 2-hour access token — this token must be refreshed before expiry and cannot be refreshed concurrently without a lock (similar to Gmail OAuth2 concurrent refresh problem). Message delivery is not guaranteed: WeChat Work has no webhook retry mechanism — if your endpoint returns non-200, the message event is permanently lost. The message type restrictions are stricter: you cannot send arbitrary markdown, only WeWork's custom markdown format (which is not standard CommonMark), and rich message cards (news type) have strict field length limits (title ≤128 chars, description ≤512 chars). The received message format includes an XML envelope (not JSON) that must be decrypted using the EncodingAESKey from the app settings before parsing. Building WeChat Work integration without reading the 企业微信开发者文档 in full is the most common source of subtle message loss bugs in Chinese market automation.

Message formatting for multi-platform bots requires a platform-aware renderer because each platform uses a different markup language. Telegram uses HTML (limited subset: <b>, <i>, <u>, <s>, <code>, <pre>, <a>, <tg-spoiler>) or MarkdownV2 (with aggressive escaping requirements — 18 characters must be escaped including '.', '!', '-', '(', ')'); Slack uses mrkdwn (its own dialect: *bold*, _italic_, `code`, >blockquote, and @mentions with <@USER_ID> syntax); Discord uses CommonMark with extensions (*bold*, **bold**, ~~strikethrough~~, ||spoiler||). The escape requirements alone are a source of constant bugs: in Telegram MarkdownV2, a URL like 'https://example.com/path-to-page' contains '-' and '.' that must be escaped inside link syntax — failing to escape causes the entire message to fail to parse and be delivered as a malformed blank. The correct pattern is a platform-agnostic intermediate representation (a document model with typed nodes: Paragraph, Bold, Code, Link, List) and platform-specific renderers that handle escaping correctly. Never do string interpolation of user-provided content directly into platform markup — it is both a formatting bug and a potential injection vector.

Long message splitting for platforms with character limits must be content-aware, not position-aware. Splitting at exactly 4096 characters (Telegram's limit) or 2000 characters (Discord's limit) breaks mid-sentence, mid-word, and mid-code-block, producing garbled output. The correct splitting algorithm: (1) attempt to split at paragraph boundaries (double newline) at or before the limit; (2) if no paragraph boundary exists within the last 20% of the limit, split at a sentence boundary (period + space + capital letter); (3) for code blocks, never split inside a code block — if the code block exceeds the limit, send it as a file attachment instead; (4) for numbered lists and bullet lists, keep each list item intact and split between items. For Telegram specifically, the message entity offsets (for bold/italic/links applied via the entities array) must be recalculated for each split chunk because they are byte offsets from the start of the message text — applying the entities from the original message to a split chunk produces wrong formatting. When splitting Markdown/HTML, ensure opening formatting tags that span a split point are closed in the first chunk and reopened in the second.

Telegram inline keyboards for bot interactions must be designed with callback_data size constraints in mind — callback_data is limited to 64 bytes per button. This constraint is routinely hit when developers try to encode full objects or URLs in callback data (e.g., a product ID + action + user context). The correct pattern is to store the full action context in Redis or a database keyed by a short ID (UUID truncated to 8 characters is sufficient for most use cases) and put only this short ID in the callback_data. The handler then looks up the full context from storage. This pattern also enables stateful keyboard flows where the same keyboard can perform different actions depending on conversation state, because the action context stored server-side can include the state at the time the keyboard was sent. For keyboards that remain valid for a long time (sent in a newsletter or pinned message), set the storage TTL to at least 30 days — short TTLs cause 'action expired' errors for users who interact with old keyboards. Always implement a graceful 'this action has expired' response rather than a generic error for expired callback data lookups.

Do not implement Telegram bot polling and webhook simultaneously for the same bot token, because Telegram's architecture treats them as mutually exclusive: if a webhook URL is set, getUpdates (polling) returns an error (409 Conflict); if polling is active, setWebhook silently fails to deliver webhooks. The common failure scenario: a developer registers a webhook in production, then runs a local polling script with the same token for debugging — the webhook deliveries stop and the production bot silently goes offline. The correct approach: use separate bot tokens for development (polling) and production (webhook). The Telegram BotFather allows creating multiple bots, and test tokens are free. Never use the production token in a development environment. Additionally, when switching from polling to webhook mode, call deleteWebhook with drop_pending_updates=True before setWebhook to clear the update queue — otherwise the webhook receives all updates that accumulated during the polling period, which can be thousands of duplicate events if the bot has been running for a while.

Slack slash command handling requires understanding that slash commands are delivered as HTTP POST requests to your endpoint (not via the Events API WebSocket) and must receive a response within 3 seconds or Slack displays a 'command failed' error to the user. For slash commands that trigger long-running operations (LLM inference, database queries, external API calls), the correct pattern is: (1) immediately return an HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing {response_type: 'in_channel', text: '⏳ Processing...'} or an empty 200 to acknowledge receipt; (2) post the actual result using the response_url provided in the slash command payload (valid for 30 minutes, allows up to 5 responses); (3) for operations taking more than 1 minute, post an intermediate 'still working' message via the response_url at 30-second intervals. The response_url pattern is more reliable than chat.postMessage for slash command responses because it is pre-authenticated and does not require looking up the channel ID. Always validate the Slack signing secret on incoming slash command requests — the signature is in the X-Slack-Signature header and prevents malicious actors from triggering your commands.

Media handling in messaging bot integrations requires platform-specific caching strategies because each platform's media URLs have different lifetime guarantees. Telegram file URLs generated via getFile are valid for only 1 hour — storing the download URL and attempting to use it later will fail with a 404. The correct pattern for Telegram: download the file immediately upon receipt and store it in your own object storage (S3, GCS), keeping only the Telegram file_id (not the URL) in your database — the file_id is permanent and can be re-fetched via getFile at any time. Discord attachment URLs use a CDN with authentication tokens that expire after an undocumented period (typically 24–48 hours). Slack file URLs require a bot token for download even if the file is 'public' — store the file_id and download on demand with the bot token. For multi-platform systems, abstract media as {platform: str, media_id: str, cached_url: str | None, cached_at: datetime | None} and implement a cache-aside pattern: check cached_url freshness before use, re-download if expired, and update the cache. Set cache TTLs conservatively (Telegram: 45 minutes, Discord: 12 hours) to stay well within platform guarantees.

Command parsing for bots must handle the Telegram-specific username suffix that appears on commands in group chats. In a group, commands are sent as '/start@YourBotName' rather than '/start' because Telegram appends the bot's username to disambiguate which bot the command is intended for when multiple bots are in the same group. A naive command parser that checks message.text == '/start' will fail in groups — the check must be message.text.startswith('/start') or (more correctly) use a regex that strips the optional @BotUsername suffix. The standard pattern: parse the command as command_text.split('@')[0].lower() to normalize. Additionally, Telegram strips the leading '/' in bot commands for inline keyboard callback payloads — ensure command handling is consistent between text commands and callback handlers. For Discord, prefix-based commands (e.g., '!help') are being superseded by slash commands which are handled via the Interactions API — building new Discord bots with prefix commands rather than slash commands means losing the platform's native command discoverability (the '/' command menu) and potentially being blocked in servers with strict permission settings that require slash commands.

Do not implement bot conversation logic that assumes messages arrive in order, because all major messaging platforms have eventual delivery guarantees with no strict ordering. Telegram guarantees that messages within a single chat are delivered in-order when using polling (because getUpdates returns updates in sequence), but webhook delivery can arrive out of order due to network variability and Telegram's own parallel delivery infrastructure. Slack explicitly documents that event delivery order is not guaranteed. Discord similarly does not guarantee event order. This creates a class of bugs in sequential workflows: a bot sends message A ('What is your name?') and expects the next message to be the answer, but message B ('Yes') from a parallel conversation arrives first. The fix is always to tie conversation context to both user_id AND a state machine step identifier, not to message arrival order. Never implement 'wait for the next message from this user' using a simple queue — always validate that the received message matches the expected state before processing it as a response to the current prompt.

Webhook signature validation must be implemented as the first middleware before any business logic processing for all messaging platform integrations. Each platform provides a different signature mechanism: Telegram uses a secret_token parameter in the setWebhook call, then includes X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token header in deliveries; Slack uses HMAC-SHA256 of (version:timestamp:body) with the signing secret, in X-Slack-Signature; Discord uses ED25519 signature verification of (timestamp + body) with the app's public key (verified via nacl library); WeChat Work uses a custom SHA1 hash of (token, timestamp, nonce, echostr). Skipping signature validation means any attacker who discovers your webhook URL can inject arbitrary messages, trigger your bot actions, exfiltrate data, or exhaust your LLM budget by flooding the endpoint with fake messages. For development, use a webhook testing tool (ngrok with request inspection, or Hookdeck) rather than disabling validation. The validation code should reject with 403 Forbidden before logging the request body — logging before validation could log malicious content that triggers log injection vulnerabilities.

Multi-platform conversation abstraction must handle the fundamental differences in conversation primitives between platforms. Telegram has three conversation types: private chat (1:1 with bot), group chat, and channel; the chat_id is negative for groups/channels and positive for private chats. Slack has channels (identified by Cxxxxxxxx), direct messages (identified by Dxxxxxxxx), and group DMs (identified by Gxxxxxxxx) — these have different permission requirements for posting. Discord has guild channels, DM channels, and threads within channels — threads have their own channel_id that must be used for thread-specific replies. WeChat Work has single-user messages and group messages with different webhook endpoints. The canonical conversation model must capture: {platform, conversation_id, conversation_type: 'dm' | 'group' | 'channel', bot_is_member: bool}. The bot_is_member field is critical — attempting to post to a Slack channel the bot is not a member of returns an error that is indistinguishable from a permission error; the bot must join the channel first via conversations.join.

Do not use Telegram Bot API long polling (getUpdates with a 60-second timeout) in an async framework (asyncio, FastAPI) without understanding that it blocks the event loop. A naive asyncio bot that calls await bot.get_updates(timeout=60) in a loop will block all other coroutines in the event loop for up to 60 seconds if no updates arrive, preventing the bot from processing other events (including health checks). The correct implementation uses python-telegram-bot's Application class (which manages long polling correctly in an async context) or implements manual polling with asyncio.wait_for and a short timeout (5 seconds), re-polling immediately on timeout. For production bots, prefer webhooks over long polling in async frameworks — the webhook pattern is inherently event-driven and does not have the blocking issue. If polling is necessary (e.g., no public endpoint for webhook), run the polling loop in a separate thread (using threading.Thread or asyncio.run_in_executor) to isolate its blocking from the rest of the async application. Benchmark polling interval carefully — a 1-second polling interval with no messages generates 86,400 empty API calls per day.

Slack Block Kit message formatting enables rich interactive messages but imposes a strict payload size limit of 50 blocks per message and a total payload size limit of 3,001 bytes for response_url messages (vs. no strict limit for chat.postMessage). Block Kit messages that exceed these limits are silently truncated — Slack returns a 200 OK but only delivers the truncated content. The practical implication: a dynamic message that lists search results (each result as a section block) must implement pagination rather than returning all results. The correct pattern for paginated Block Kit responses: include 'Previous' and 'Next' button actions (Actions block) with the page offset encoded in the action value, and store the full result set server-side keyed by a session ID in the action value. For messages that may have variable length content (LLM responses, document summaries), implement a character budget check before constructing the Block Kit payload — count the characters in each text field and truncate with an ellipsis at 3,000 characters total across all text fields before building the blocks array. Never discover the truncation limit through production failures; test with max-length inputs during development.

Discord application commands (slash commands) must handle the 15-minute deferred response window correctly for long-running operations. When a slash command triggers work that takes more than 3 seconds, you must immediately call interaction.defer_response() (which sends an initial 'thinking...' state to the user) and then follow up with interaction.followup.send() within 15 minutes. After 15 minutes, the interaction token expires and any followup attempt returns a 401. For operations that may take more than 15 minutes (batch processing, large file operations), the correct pattern is to defer immediately, start the long operation as a background task, and send the result as a new message via channel.send() rather than via the interaction followup — channel.send() does not have the 15-minute expiry. Always monitor the time elapsed between defer and followup in production — background task queue backlogs can silently push operations past the 15-minute window, causing the user to see a 'thinking...' indicator that never resolves. Set an explicit task timeout at 14 minutes that sends an 'operation timed out' followup message rather than letting the interaction expire silently.

Bot user identification across platforms requires normalizing user IDs into a platform-scoped namespace to prevent ID collisions. User ID '12345' on Telegram is a different person than user ID '12345' on Slack, but if both are stored in the same users table with just the integer ID, queries will return wrong users. The canonical user identifier must always include the platform prefix: 'telegram:12345', 'slack:U12345678', 'discord:12345678901234567'. For systems that support cross-platform user linkage (a user who is on both Slack and Telegram), maintain a separate identity_links table with (platform_a, user_id_a, platform_b, user_id_b, linked_at, linked_by_method) rather than trying to unify IDs — linked identities have their own lifecycle (can be unlinked, can be incorrect) that must be managed separately from the core user record. Never auto-link users across platforms based solely on display name similarity — display names are user-controlled and not unique within a platform. Only link on explicit user action (user sends the same linking code on both platforms) or on verified shared identifiers (same verified email address on both platforms).

Do not implement Slack bot posting without understanding the bot token vs. user token distinction, because they have different capabilities and different audit trail implications. Bot tokens (xoxb-) can only post as the bot identity; user tokens (xoxp-) allow posting on behalf of a human user but require explicit user OAuth consent and are subject to stricter Slack audit log scrutiny (enterprise organizations log all user token API calls for compliance). Using a user token for automation posting creates compliance issues in regulated industries (FINRA, HIPAA) because messages appear to come from a human but were generated by automation — regulators treat this as deceptive communication. The correct architecture for automated messages is always bot tokens, with the bot's identity clearly named (e.g., 'SalesBot' or 'AlertBot') so recipients understand they are interacting with automation. For workspace-level operations that require elevated permissions (managing channels, accessing private channels), use user tokens only for the administrative setup steps (creating channels, inviting members) and bot tokens for all ongoing message operations. Scope each token to the minimum required permissions.

Telegram webhook setup for production requires configuring the max_connections parameter carefully, because the default of 40 connections means Telegram can open up to 40 simultaneous webhook POST requests to your server. For a bot handling LLM responses (each taking 2–8 seconds), 40 simultaneous connections means 40 concurrent LLM calls — which can exhaust your LLM API rate limits and your server's memory simultaneously. The setWebhook call accepts max_connections (1–100) to control this. Calculate the correct value: max_connections = floor(LLM_rate_limit_rps * average_response_time_seconds), with a 20% safety margin. Additionally, Telegram webhook endpoints must use HTTPS with a valid SSL certificate from a trusted CA (self-signed certs are supported but require uploading the certificate to Telegram via setWebhook's certificate parameter). The webhook URL must be on port 443, 80, 88, or 8443 — other ports are silently rejected. For bots that need to process messages sequentially per user (to maintain conversation coherence), implement per-user message queuing in your webhook handler even though Telegram delivers them in parallel — allow_updates parameter can filter to specific update types, reducing irrelevant webhook calls.

Multi-platform bot deployment must handle the different reconnection behavior of persistent gateway connections vs. stateless webhooks. Discord and Slack's Real-Time Messaging (RTM, now deprecated in favor of Events API) use persistent WebSocket connections that require active reconnection logic: WebSocket connections drop due to network interruptions, server-side session expirations (Discord invalidates sessions after 24 hours), and rate limit-induced disconnections. The correct WebSocket reconnection pattern: (1) implement an exponential backoff reconnect with jitter (start at 1 second, cap at 5 minutes); (2) for Discord, use the 'resume' mechanism (send Resume payload with session_id and seq) rather than full reconnect to avoid re-processing missed events; (3) monitor WebSocket heartbeat ACKs — if the server stops sending heartbeat ACKs, close and reconnect immediately rather than waiting for TCP timeout; (4) set a maximum reconnect attempt count (e.g., 10) after which the process restarts entirely rather than entering an infinite reconnect loop that masks deeper issues. For production deployments, use the maintained library (discord.py, slack-sdk) rather than raw WebSocket — they implement this reconnection logic correctly.

Do not implement bot rate limiting by sleeping (time.sleep) between API calls, because sleep-based throttling blocks the entire thread/process and prevents handling incoming messages, health checks, and other concurrent operations. In a bot handling multiple simultaneous conversations, sleeping to respect a rate limit for one user's request blocks all other users for the duration of the sleep. The correct rate limiting implementation for messaging bots uses a non-blocking token bucket or leaky bucket algorithm: maintain a queue of outbound requests with timestamps; before sending, check if the token bucket has available tokens (refilled at the allowed rate); if no tokens, enqueue the request and use asyncio.sleep (non-blocking) or schedule the send for when the next token is available. For asyncio bots, use asyncio.Queue with a consumer that paces sends. The most common production failure mode: a bot successfully handles 100 messages/minute for months, then a viral event generates 1,000 messages/minute — a sleep-based throttle serializes all processing and creates a growing backlog; a proper async queue maintains responsiveness by shedding load gracefully.

Slack app distribution via OAuth (allowing other workspaces to install your app) requires implementing the complete OAuth 2.0 installation flow with state parameter CSRF protection, which is different from the simpler bot token creation for single-workspace bots. The installation flow: (1) generate a cryptographically random state token (32 bytes, hex-encoded); (2) store it in a session cookie with a 10-minute expiry; (3) redirect the user to Slack's authorization URL with state parameter; (4) on the callback, verify the state parameter matches the session cookie before exchanging the code for a token; (5) exchange the authorization code for an access token via oauth.v2.access (this returns both a bot token AND optionally a user token if user scopes were requested); (6) store the bot token per workspace_id with encryption at rest. The critical security step is the state verification — without it, a CSRF attack can trick a workspace admin into installing your app on their workspace, granting your app access to their Slack. For multi-workspace SaaS bots, implement token rotation: when Slack rotates bot tokens (for security incidents), they deliver the new token via the tokens_revoked event — handle this event to update stored tokens.

Bot error handling for messaging platforms must distinguish between user-facing errors and system errors, and handle each differently. User-facing errors (invalid input, unsupported command, missing permissions) should produce a helpful message in the chat that explains what went wrong and what the user can do. System errors (API failures, database unavailability, LLM errors) should produce a minimal user-facing message ('Something went wrong, please try again in a moment') while logging the full error with context (user_id, platform, message_id, stack trace) for operator investigation. Never send raw exception messages to users — they leak implementation details, can contain sensitive information (file paths, database connection strings in error messages), and produce a poor user experience. For Telegram, send errors as ephemeral messages in the conversation; for Slack, use ephemeral messages (chat.postEphemeral) for user errors so other channel members do not see them; for Discord, use ephemeral interaction responses (ephemeral=True in the response). Implement a 'bot health' monitoring that alerts operators when the error rate exceeds 5% of processed messages in any 10-minute window.

Testing messaging bot integrations requires a deterministic test harness that does not make real API calls, because real platform APIs have rate limits, require authentication, and produce non-deterministic responses (message IDs, timestamps, user IDs) that make tests flaky. The correct testing architecture uses a 'fake platform transport' that implements the same interface as the real platform client but stores sent messages in memory and allows test code to inject incoming messages programmatically. For Telegram, the fake transport intercepts sendMessage calls and records them as (chat_id, text, parse_mode, reply_markup) tuples that test assertions check; for incoming messages, it provides a send_update(update_dict) method that triggers the handler pipeline as if a real update arrived. This approach tests the full bot logic (conversation state transitions, command parsing, error handling, rate limiting) without any network calls. Additionally, implement contract tests that run against the real platform API in a dedicated test bot/workspace — these run nightly (not on every commit) to catch platform API changes. Never use the production bot token in tests — credential exposure and accidental real-message delivery are both unacceptable outcomes.

Telegram bot architecture for production systems requires using webhooks with HTTPS endpoints rather than long-polling in all scenarios where the server has a public IP. Polling consumes a persistent connection and is limited to one concurrent receiver; webhooks allow horizontal scaling and sub-100ms message delivery. The setup: register a webhook URL via the setWebhook API call with a valid HTTPS certificate (Telegram requires a valid CA certificate, not self-signed); handle incoming Updates via POST to your webhook endpoint; respond with 200 within 60 seconds or Telegram will retry. The retry behavior: Telegram resends unacknowledged webhooks up to 5 times with increasing delays. The idempotency requirement: your handler must be idempotent because the same Update can arrive multiple times. Use the Update.update_id as the deduplication key, stored in Redis with 24-hour TTL.

Do not process incoming Slack messages synchronously within the 3-second Slack event acknowledgement window, because any processing that takes more than 3 seconds will cause Slack to retry the event delivery up to 3 times, resulting in duplicate message handling. The failure cascade: a bot that calls an LLM API (typically 2–15 seconds) within the event handler will timeout; Slack retries; the LLM call is made multiple times; the bot sends multiple identical responses to the user. The correct architecture: respond to Slack's Events API POST with HTTP 200 immediately; enqueue the event payload for async processing; process the event in a separate worker that posts results back via the Slack Web API. Use the Slack message timestamp (ts) as the idempotency key to prevent duplicate responses when the async worker retries.

WhatsApp Business API message templates require pre-approval by Meta before sending to customers who haven't messaged you first (outside the 24-hour customer service window). This is not a bureaucratic obstacle — it is Meta's spam prevention mechanism that maintains WhatsApp's high deliverability and open rates (98% open rate vs. 20% for email). The approval process: submit template text with parameter placeholders to Meta; Meta reviews within 24–72 hours; approved templates can be sent to opted-in users at any time. The practical constraint: any new outbound notification category requires template pre-approval. Plan 1-week lead time for template approval before launching any new WhatsApp notification workflow. The business consequence of sending non-template messages outside the 24-hour window: the API returns an error and the message is not delivered — there is no workaround.

Discord bot integration for community automation requires using Discord's slash commands (Application Commands) rather than message prefix parsing, because slash commands are first-class API features with built-in input validation, auto-complete, and permission control, while prefix parsing is fragile (breaks on message formatting, bot mentions, etc.). The implementation: register commands via the Application Commands API at startup (global commands propagate in up to 1 hour; guild-specific commands propagate immediately); handle INTERACTION_CREATE events in your webhook handler; respond with InteractionResponse within 3 seconds or defer with a thinking indicator for long operations. The permission model: slash commands support required permissions at the command level, reducing the need for manual permission checks in the handler code. Always use ephemeral responses (visible=false) for responses to commands that should not clutter the channel (error messages, status checks, private confirmations).

Do not send WhatsApp messages to phone numbers without explicit opt-in verification, because the WhatsApp Business API terms require that all recipients have explicitly opted in to receive messages from your business. Sending to numbers without opt-in results in: (1) recipients marking messages as spam; (2) Meta suspending the phone number's WhatsApp Business API access; (3) potential legal violations of telecommunications regulations in the recipient's country (TCPA in the US, GDPR in the EU for contact initiation). The opt-in must be obtained through a method the recipient controls — a web form, an in-app toggle, or an explicit verbal agreement with recorded consent. Store the opt-in consent metadata (timestamp, source, phone number) as you would email consent. Recovery from a suspended WhatsApp number requires a manual appeal process that takes days to weeks.

Multi-platform messaging integration (sending notifications via Slack, Teams, SMS, email, Telegram based on user preference) requires an abstraction layer (notification channel router) that separates the message content from the delivery mechanism. The correct architecture: define a Notification object with content, urgency_level, and notification_type; the router reads the user's channel preferences and urgency thresholds from a settings store; routes the notification to the correct channel adapter. Channel adapters are independently testable and replaceable. Without this abstraction, channel-specific logic (Slack formatting, SMS length limit, Telegram parse_mode) bleeds into business logic, making it impossible to add or replace channels without modifying core application code. The urgency threshold is a key routing decision: high-urgency alerts go to SMS even if the user prefers Slack, because SMS has better interruption characteristics for urgent situations.

Microsoft Teams bot framework integration requires registering through Azure Bot Service, not direct API calls, because Teams enforces a strict security model that validates bot identity through Azure AD. Unlike Slack (which uses signing secrets for webhook validation) or Telegram (which uses bot tokens), Teams requires the bot to hold an Azure AD application identity and exchange JWT tokens for each API call. This creates an additional setup step that many developers miss: the bot must be registered in Azure Portal, the App ID and password must be configured in the bot's environment, and the bot must implement the Bot Framework authentication flow. The practical consequence: testing Teams bots locally requires either ngrok tunneling with the correct Azure registration or using the Bot Framework Emulator. Attempting to call Teams APIs directly without this auth flow produces 401 errors that can be misdiagnosed as API access issues.

Do not store messaging platform credentials (Slack bot tokens, WhatsApp API keys, Telegram bot tokens) directly in application configuration files or environment variable files committed to version control, because these credentials provide full access to the bot's identity and all message content the bot can see. A leaked Slack bot token allows reading all channel messages the bot is a member of and sending messages as the bot to all members. Rotate any credential that was committed to version control immediately — treat it as compromised regardless of repository visibility. The correct credential management: store credentials in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or equivalent); inject them at runtime via environment variables from the secrets manager; rotate credentials quarterly and on personnel changes. Messaging platform APIs provide no way to audit past API calls made with a compromised credential — you cannot know what data was exfiltrated.

Telegram bot rate limiting requires implementing token bucket rate limiting at the application level because Telegram's API enforces rate limits that result in 429 errors when exceeded: 30 messages per second globally, 20 messages per minute to the same chat. The practical approach for high-volume bots: use a priority queue where messages are held until the rate limiter has capacity; implement exponential backoff starting at 1 second when a 429 is received (Telegram includes a retry_after field in the error response specifying the minimum wait time); process different chats concurrently up to the global 30/second limit. For bulk notification systems sending to many chats simultaneously: spread sends over time using a scheduler, target 15 messages/second to leave headroom for organic bot traffic. Hitting the per-chat limit (20/minute) is more common than the global limit and causes confusion — implement per-chat queuing separately from the global rate limiter.

Slack message formatting requires choosing between Block Kit (rich components: sections, images, buttons, input fields) and simple markdown depending on the use case. Block Kit is required for interactive components (buttons, dropdowns, date pickers) and produces consistent rendering across Slack clients; simple markdown renders differently across desktop vs. mobile clients and cannot include interactive elements. The practical decision rule: use Block Kit for all bot-initiated messages that require user interaction (approvals, form collection, selection); use simple markdown only for informational messages where interaction is not needed. The operational cost of using simple markdown when Block Kit is appropriate: interactive patterns implemented via emoji reactions or DM commands are fragile and don't work reliably when users are on mobile. Block Kit messages require more code to construct but produce significantly better UX, especially on mobile where button tap targets are much more usable than reaction emoji.

SMS/MMS messaging via Twilio, AWS SNS, or similar carriers has content constraints that differ from internet messaging: (1) a single SMS is 160 characters for ASCII or 70 characters for Unicode (any character outside ASCII causes the entire message to be split into 70-character segments); (2) concatenated messages (longer than one segment) require a 6-byte UDH header per segment, reducing available content per segment; (3) MMS supports up to 1600KB per message but not all carriers support all content types; (4) carrier filtering automatically blocks messages containing certain keywords (buy, free, win in certain contexts) or patterns (all caps, excessive punctuation). For AI-generated SMS content: validate character count before sending; check for Unicode characters that trigger shorter segment limits; run through a carrier filter keyword checker; ensure opt-in language appears in first message to a new number. SMS costs scale with segment count, not message count — unexpected Unicode characters can triple SMS costs.

Building a cross-platform messaging bot requires handling the different user identity systems per platform: Slack uses workspace-specific user IDs (same person has different IDs in different workspaces); Telegram uses permanent user IDs; Discord uses permanent user IDs but allows anonymous interactions; WhatsApp uses phone numbers as identifiers. The correct user identity architecture: maintain an internal user table with your system's user_id as primary; create a messaging_accounts join table with (user_id, platform, platform_user_id) mapping. When a user interacts via any platform, look up their platform_user_id in the join table to get the system user_id, then proceed with system-level logic. Without this mapping layer, the same user interacting via Slack and WhatsApp appears as two different users, losing conversation history and preference continuity. Implement the mapping table from day one — retrofitting it into a running system requires migrating all existing state.

Do not implement Slack app home tabs with heavy data loading on every open event, because the app_home_opened event fires every time any user opens the home tab — even just switching to it momentarily. An app with 1000 users that makes 3 external API calls per app_home_opened event makes 3000 API calls per minute during business hours if 100 users have the tab visible. The correct pattern: cache the home tab view content per user with a reasonable TTL (5 minutes for most content, 30 seconds for time-sensitive dashboards); only make external API calls when the cache is expired; serve the cached view immediately and refresh in the background. The Slack home tab architecture should use a background refresh trigger (scheduled job or webhook from data source) rather than on-demand data loading per user view event.

Setting up WhatsApp Business API via Meta Cloud API requires: (1) create a Meta developer account and a Meta app; (2) enable the WhatsApp Business API product in the app; (3) verify your business in Meta Business Manager (requires business documents, takes 1–7 days); (4) add a phone number and go through the phone number verification process; (5) set up a webhook endpoint that accepts POST requests and validates the x-hub-signature-256 header using your webhook verification token; (6) register the webhook in the Meta app dashboard for the messages, message_status, and template_status events; (7) test by sending a message from the Telegram interface to your test number and verifying the webhook receives it. The most commonly missed step: step 5 (webhook signature validation). Meta sends the hash of the payload in the x-hub-signature-256 header — validate this before processing any webhook content to prevent spoofed webhook attacks.

Conversation context management for multi-turn chat bots must use a sliding window approach with semantic compression for long conversations. Storing the full conversation history and injecting it into every LLM call grows the context linearly — by turn 50, the context window is mostly conversation history rather than the current task. The correct approach: maintain a compressed summary of the conversation updated every 5 turns ('the user asked about X, we established Y, their main goal is Z'); combine the summary with the last 3–5 messages as the LLM context. This keeps context size constant regardless of conversation length. The summary update step: use a cheap, fast model (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku) to generate the summary update, not the expensive model used for response generation. For business-critical conversations: persist the full conversation log in the database even while using the compressed context for LLM calls — the full log is needed for human review and debugging.

Slack workspace message retention policies configured by the workspace admin can delete messages older than N days, which affects bots that store conversation state in Slack messages or threads. A bot that relies on retrieving historical messages via conversations.history to reconstruct conversation context will fail silently for conversations older than the retention window — it will receive an empty or partial history without an obvious error. The correct architecture: never rely on Slack's message history as the persistent state store for your bot; maintain all conversation state in your own database; use message timestamps (ts) as foreign keys to correlate Slack messages with your stored state, but treat the Slack message content as ephemeral. This architecture also enables replying to messages even when the original message was deleted, and allows conversation continuity across workspace migrations.

Chatbot conversation design for task-completion workflows should minimize the number of turns required to complete the workflow, because each turn increases dropout rate — users who start a multi-step bot flow abandon it at each step. Measured dropout rates: 15–25% dropout per step for text-input flows; 5–10% dropout per step for button-selection flows. For a 5-step flow: text input has 54–77% completion rate; button selection has 59–77% completion rate. The design implication: collapse multiple fields into a single turn using structured input (modals, forms) where the platform supports it; use smart defaults that pre-fill likely values so the user only needs to confirm, not re-enter; put the highest-friction step last so users who have invested in earlier steps are more likely to complete. For LLM-powered chatbots: use natural language understanding to collect multiple intents in a single conversational turn rather than asking separate questions for each attribute.

Message template versioning for WhatsApp Business requires tracking which version of a template was sent with each message for compliance and debugging purposes. WhatsApp templates can be updated (by creating a new template with a modified version) but the old template remains valid until explicitly deleted. Recipients who received message A from template v1 may receive follow-up message B from template v2 — without version tracking, the follow-up message may reference the wrong template parameters if the template structure changed. The implementation: store (message_id, template_name, template_version) with each sent message; when sending follow-up messages in a conversation, verify that the template version used for the initial message is still active; if the template was updated since the conversation started, review the follow-up message content for consistency.

Do not use Slack incoming webhooks for interactive message workflows that require user responses, because incoming webhooks are one-way only — they can send messages but cannot receive user interactions or replies. A common mistake: using an incoming webhook to send a message with buttons, then trying to handle the button click through the same webhook URL. Button clicks require an interactive_url or response_action endpoint registered separately from the webhook. The correct integration stack for interactive Slack messages: use the Slack Web API (not incoming webhooks) to post messages; configure a separate interactive endpoint in your Slack app settings to receive payload callbacks from button clicks and modal submissions. Incoming webhooks are appropriate only for one-way notifications (alerts, status updates, summaries) where no user interaction is needed.

Production Slack integration for internal tooling assembles as: (1) Event subscription webhook with HTTPS endpoint and signing secret validation; (2) Interactive components endpoint for button/modal callbacks; (3) Slash command handler for explicit bot invocations; (4) Async processing queue (Redis/SQS) that decouples event receipt from processing; (5) State management store (PostgreSQL or Redis) for conversation context and user preferences; (6) Slack Web API client with retry logic and rate limit awareness; (7) Block Kit message composer for interactive UX; (8) OAuth2 flow for workspace-level installation and token management. The components that are most commonly implemented incorrectly: step 4 (sync instead of async, leading to timeout-and-retry loops) and step 6 (no rate limiting, leading to 429 errors during burst). The entire integration is testable locally using Slack's Socket Mode for development, switching to webhook mode for production.

Telegram bot user permission system should mirror the user roles from your application rather than inventing a separate bot-level permission system. The implementation: when a user first interacts with the bot, link their Telegram user_id to their application account (via a one-time link token sent via email); thereafter, permission checks use the application's existing role/permission model via the linked account. The common anti-pattern: building a parallel permission system inside the bot (storing admin user IDs in a config file) that diverges from the application's permissions over time, creating security inconsistencies where a user who was revoked application access can still perform administrative actions via the bot. Single source of truth for permissions prevents this class of vulnerability.

Do not broadcast messages to all users of a messaging platform bot simultaneously, because broadcasting to a large user base creates a send storm that: (1) exhausts rate limits immediately; (2) creates a thundering herd effect when users respond simultaneously; (3) with Telegram's 30 msg/sec global limit, sending to 10,000 users takes at least 5.5 minutes for the broadcast to complete. The correct broadcast architecture: implement a scheduled sender that processes the user list in batches with configurable delay between batches (typically 25 messages/second to stay under limits); maintain broadcast state (which users have been sent, which are pending, failures) persistently so the broadcast can be resumed after interruption; implement unsubscribe enforcement by checking the current subscription status for each user before send, not at the start of the broadcast.

Message queuing between messaging platforms and backend processing should use at-least-once delivery semantics, not at-most-once, because lost messages in customer-facing communication are higher cost than duplicate messages. A missed support request or order confirmation represents real customer impact; a duplicate message that the idempotency layer deduplicates has zero customer impact. The practical implementation: configure your message queue (SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka) with at-least-once delivery; implement idempotency at the processing layer using the platform message ID as the dedup key; set the visibility timeout (SQS) or consumer acknowledgement (RabbitMQ) to exceed your processing time by 2x to prevent premature redelivery. For messaging integrations specifically: the idempotency check should happen before any external API call (sending a response), not after — deduplicate incoming messages early to prevent duplicate outbound responses.

Line messaging API integration for Southeast Asian markets requires handling Line's unique features: (1) rich menus (persistent bottom UI panels with up to 20 tap areas that replace keyboard for common actions); (2) flex messages (CSS-like layout engine for structured messages); (3) LIFF (Line Front-end Framework) for in-chat mini web apps; (4) Line Login for authentication without redirecting users to a browser. The rich menu is Line's most powerful differentiation from other messaging platforms — users see the menu on every conversation open, enabling always-available quick actions without users needing to know commands. For production Line integrations: invest in the rich menu design (created via the Messaging API as a background image + area definitions), as it significantly reduces support volume for common user actions.

Messenger (Facebook Messenger) has deprecated many API features since the Meta pivot to WhatsApp Business: (1) the sponsored messages API was retired; (2) many message types have been deprecated; (3) access to user profile information has been significantly restricted. Current Messenger API capabilities are primarily limited to: (a) responding to messages from users who initiate contact; (b) sending notifications within the 24-hour customer service window; (c) using approved message tags for specific use cases outside the window (post-purchase updates, account alerts, human agent handoff). For new messaging integration projects in 2024+: evaluate whether the Messenger API still supports your intended use case before investing in integration — the platform has been deprioritized by Meta and capabilities have been reduced.

Chatbot fallback handling requires a structured escalation path when the bot fails to understand a user's message after 2–3 attempts. The escalation sequence: (1) on first failure to understand: ask for clarification in a different way ('I didn't catch that — could you rephrase?'); (2) on second failure: offer a structured menu of the most common options ('I'm having trouble understanding. What would you like to do? [Option A / Option B / Talk to a human]'); (3) on third failure or explicit 'talk to human' request: hand off to human support with full conversation context forwarded. The failure to implement this escalation path causes the bot to loop indefinitely on unhandled inputs, generating user frustration that results in complaint messages and negative reviews. The handoff to human support is the safety valve — it must always be available as a fallback option regardless of the bot's confidence in understanding the input.

Do not implement a chatbot that changes its behavior based on whether it thinks a user is testing it vs. using it for real, because this creates inconsistent experiences that erode trust when discovered. The test-detection failure mode: a bot that performs well in manual testing but fails in production because the test environment uses different credentials, different feature flags, or different data conditions. The correct approach: treat test traffic identically to production traffic (use a separate test bot account to avoid polluting production data, but use identical code paths); if the bot is deployed in a staging environment, ensure the staging environment data is representative of production (size, diversity, edge cases). Any behavioral difference between test and production environments is a potential production bug that was never caught in testing.

Messaging integration analytics must track conversation outcomes, not just message volume, because message count is a vanity metric that tells you nothing about whether the bot is delivering value. The outcome metrics that matter: (1) task completion rate per conversation type (did the user accomplish what they came to do?); (2) escalation to human rate (high rate = bot is failing users; sudden drop = bot is blocking escalation it shouldn't); (3) user retention (do users return to the bot after their first interaction?); (4) error rate per intent (which intents are handled poorly?); (5) response confidence distribution (what percentage of intents are handled with high confidence?). The tracking implementation: add explicit 'task completed' and 'task abandoned' events to the conversation flow at logical completion points; instrument every intent handler with a confidence score log; send these events to your analytics platform for dashboarding.

Multi-channel messaging automation hub assembles as: (1) channel adapters for each platform (Slack, Teams, Telegram, WhatsApp, SMS) normalizing incoming messages to a unified Message schema; (2) user identity resolver matching platform user IDs to system users; (3) intent classifier (LLM-based) determining the user's goal and required action; (4) action router dispatching to the appropriate business logic handler; (5) state manager maintaining per-user conversation context across channels; (6) response composer generating channel-appropriate responses (formatting, length, interactive elements per platform capabilities); (7) delivery manager handling platform-specific sending with rate limits, retry, and delivery confirmation; (8) analytics collector emitting conversation events for outcome tracking. The key architectural principle: all business logic operates on system-level user IDs and platform-agnostic Message objects — no platform-specific code in the business logic layer. This architecture allows adding a new messaging channel by implementing only a new channel adapter without modifying business logic.

Notification priority routing across messaging channels requires a severity matrix that maps alert severity to delivery channel based on time-of-day and user availability signals. The matrix: CRITICAL alerts (production down, security incident) → SMS + phone call + Slack regardless of time; HIGH alerts (performance degradation, payment failures) → Slack + email during business hours, SMS during off-hours; MEDIUM alerts (warning thresholds, non-critical errors) → Slack during business hours, email if off-hours; LOW alerts (informational, trend reports) → email only. User availability signals: calendar integration can show when a user is in a meeting (suppress non-critical messages); Slack status can indicate 'do not disturb' (respect this for non-critical notifications). The most important design decision: the CRITICAL threshold must be set conservatively — alert fatigue from too-frequent CRITICAL alerts causes users to disable or ignore all alerts.

Do not use message delivery timestamps from messaging platforms as the authoritative timestamp for business logic (e.g., 'message received at timestamp X triggers deadline Y'), because delivery timestamps reflect when the platform processed the message, not when the user sent it. Users can send messages from offline mode (stored locally, delivered when connection restores), from different timezones, or through third-party clients that add latency. The business-logic-safe timestamp is the one your backend records when it processes the webhook — this is under your system's control and clock. Store both the platform-reported timestamp (for conversation display) and the processing timestamp (for business logic) separately. The failure mode is particularly problematic for time-sensitive workflows (SLA calculations, auction deadlines, first-response metrics) where a user who sent a message before a deadline appears to have missed it due to delivery delay.

Bot-to-bot coordination in multi-agent messaging systems requires explicit coordination protocols rather than emergent behavior from shared channels. The failure mode without protocols: two bots monitoring the same Slack channel both respond to the same user message within milliseconds of each other, creating confusing duplicate or contradictory responses. The coordination mechanism: designate one bot per channel as the 'responder' and all others as 'observers'; the responder claims the right to respond by posting a processing indicator (typing indicator or a reserved reaction emoji) before generating the full response; observers check for the claim indicator before responding. Alternatively, use direct message routing: all user messages go to a dedicated orchestrator bot that routes to the appropriate specialized bot and aggregates responses. The shared-channel coordination approach is fragile and should only be used when routing is impractical.

WhatsApp's 24-hour customer service window is a fundamental architectural constraint that must be built into every workflow. After a user initiates contact, your bot has a 24-hour window to send any messages — not just replies to specific messages. After 24 hours of user inactivity, you can only send pre-approved template messages. This constraint changes the bot architecture: for workflows that span multiple days (multi-step onboarding, extended support cases), you must use template messages for any message sent outside the window, which requires planning which templates you'll need for each workflow before launch. The design pattern: identify all messages your bot might need to send more than 24 hours after the last user interaction; pre-approve templates for each; at send time, check whether the user last interacted within 24 hours and select free-form vs. template accordingly.

Telegram bot Token security requires rotating tokens after any potential exposure, because Telegram bot tokens have no expiry and provide permanent access to the bot's identity. The rotation procedure: call the bot.token_revoke API (not officially documented but supported) or use BotFather to generate a new token; update all deployments with the new token simultaneously (minimizing window where old token is still configured); verify old token is rejected (attempt an API call with the old token — should return 401). The deployment coordination challenge: if the bot runs in multiple instances (horizontal scaling), all instances must switch to the new token in coordination to prevent mixed-token state. Implement token rotation as a zero-downtime procedure using a blue-green deployment pattern where the new token is deployed to the new fleet before the old fleet is shut down.

Conversational AI for customer service in messaging apps requires explicit confidence gating: high-confidence intent + high-confidence entity extraction → automated response; medium-confidence → automated response with human review flag; low-confidence → immediate escalation to human agent with full context. The confidence is multidimensional: intent classification confidence AND entity extraction completeness AND policy compliance check. A message that is high-confidence intent but missing a required entity (account number, order ID) should not be automated — the missing entity must be requested explicitly. For implementation: define a minimum entity completeness score per intent type (some intents require zero entities; others require specific entities); gate automated responses on both intent confidence and entity completeness. Automating incomplete interactions causes the bot to make decisions with insufficient information, generating incorrect outcomes that require costly human remediation.

Do not implement messaging integrations that store conversation transcripts in the messaging platform (using message pinning, channel archives, or thread comments as the data store) because this creates a data retention and access control problem. Messaging platforms have their own data deletion and compliance policies that may not match your business requirements — Slack can delete messages based on workspace admin policy; WhatsApp messages may be deleted by users; Telegram bot messages can be deleted by users after the fact. Any transcript data that needs to be preserved for compliance, legal holds, or business analysis must be written to your own data store at the time of receipt, not retrieved from the messaging platform later. The real-time capture rule: store every message your bot receives and sends in your database with the platform message ID as the external reference; treat the messaging platform as a delivery mechanism only, not a data store.

Setting up Slack OAuth2 for multi-workspace bot installation requires: (1) configure OAuth scopes in Slack app settings (minimum: chat:write, app_mentions:read); (2) implement the OAuth flow: redirect user to Slack's OAuth endpoint with your client_id and scopes; (3) handle the callback: exchange the authorization code for an access token via oauth.v2.access; (4) store the access token and bot_user_id per workspace in your database (key: team_id); (5) use the workspace-specific token for all API calls to that workspace; (6) handle token revocation: subscribe to the app_uninstalled event and delete the stored token. The critical detail most miss: Slack access tokens are workspace-scoped, not user-scoped. A single bot installation gives you one bot token that works for all users in that workspace. Storing tokens per-user instead of per-workspace wastes storage and creates management overhead without any benefit.

Real-time typing indicators ('bot is typing...') in messaging integrations must be used judiciously: show them for operations taking more than 2 seconds (LLM generation, database queries); do not show them for instant responses (command acknowledgements, cached lookups). The implementation for Telegram: sendChatAction with 'typing' action, which displays for 5 seconds before auto-clearing — resend every 4 seconds during long operations to keep the indicator active. For Slack: use the chat.postMessage with blocks containing a loading indicator, then update the message when content is ready using chat.update. The UX rationale: typing indicators set expectations and prevent users from sending duplicate requests while waiting — without them, users who don't see a response after 3 seconds will often send the same request again, creating duplicate processing queues.

Messaging bots serving multiple languages must perform language detection at the message level, not at the user profile level, because users frequently switch languages mid-conversation (sending in their native language when they don't know a technical term in English, switching to English for code examples). Profile-level language settings will serve the wrong language for these mixed-language interactions. The correct approach: detect language on each incoming message using a fast language detector (fastText's language identification model achieves 98% accuracy in <1ms); route to the appropriate language model or translate to English for processing; respond in the detected language of the most recent message. The only exception: highly structured workflows (form collection, menu selection) where consistency is more important than language following.

AI customer support bot for e-commerce assembles as: (1) Platform adapters: Telegram, WhatsApp, and website chat widget — all routing to the same backend; (2) Intent classification: order status, return request, product inquiry, shipping question, complaint; (3) Entity extraction: order_id, product_sku, email, phone (from message or user profile); (4) API integration layer: order management API, shipping carrier API, product catalog; (5) Response generation: RAG-based responses using product FAQ and policy documents as context; (6) Human escalation: direct handoff to support agent for complaint and unrecognized intents; (7) Feedback collection: thumbs up/down on each automated response; (8) Analytics: completion rate, escalation rate, CSAT per intent category. Integrating real order and shipping data (step 4) is the component that converts a generic chatbot into a genuinely useful customer service tool — without it, the bot can only give generic answers that don't address the customer's specific situation.

Discord message limits are 2000 characters per message, 6000 characters for embed description, and 25 fields per embed. For AI-generated responses that may exceed 2000 characters: split at the nearest sentence boundary before the limit; send as separate messages in sequence; or use Discord embeds for structured content with pagination. The alternative for very long responses (technical documentation, reports): generate the content as a file (Markdown, PDF) and attach it to the message — files up to 25MB can be sent. The failure to respect character limits results in a 400 error with no partial delivery — the entire message is rejected. For LLM-generated Discord responses: add a post-processing step that checks message length and splits if necessary before attempting to send.

Messaging integration testing must include simulated high-load scenarios to catch rate-limiting and state management issues before production. The test scenarios that reveal the most common production failures: (1) 100 concurrent users sending the same message simultaneously (tests rate limiting and idempotency); (2) a single user sending 50 messages in rapid succession (tests per-user rate limiting and conversation state consistency); (3) a user whose session expires mid-workflow (tests state recovery and re-authentication flows); (4) a webhook that is delivered twice with identical payload (tests idempotency key handling); (5) a message arriving after the user's session has been deleted (tests graceful handling of orphaned messages). Tools for load testing messaging integrations: Locust (Python) for Slack/HTTP-based platforms; custom scripts using the platform's test environment for WhatsApp and Telegram. The concurrent user test (#1) is the most commonly skipped and the most commonly triggered production issue.

Slack channel architecture for team automation bots should separate notification channels from interactive command channels. A single #general-bot channel that receives both automated alerts and processes user commands creates noise pollution that reduces the utility of both. The correct architecture: dedicated notification channels per alert category (e.g., #alerts-infra, #alerts-billing); a dedicated #bot-commands channel for interactive workflows; private DMs for sensitive operations that shouldn't be visible to the whole team. The channel routing logic should be configured in the bot's settings, not hardcoded — channel IDs change when workspaces are migrated or reorganized. Store channel IDs in a configuration database keyed by channel purpose (notifications.billing, commands.general) and resolve the actual channel ID at send time.

Do not implement SMS two-way messaging (where users can reply to SMS notifications) without handling the stop/start/help standard keyword responses required by US carrier regulations. TCPA requires that SMS programs honor STOP to immediately stop all future messages from that number, START to re-enable messages after a STOP, and HELP to send your program name and contact information. Carriers automatically intercept these keywords on shortcodes — but for longcodes and 10DLC numbers, your application must handle them. Failure to honor STOP commands is a TCPA violation with statutory damages of $500–$1500 per violation. The implementation: configure keyword responses in your Twilio account or equivalent; in your inbound message handler, check for STOP/START/HELP before routing to your bot logic; write the opt-out status to your suppression list before any response.

Telegram bot command menu design should follow the single-responsibility principle: each command does one thing clearly. The /start command must only show the welcome message and options; the /help command must only explain available commands; operational commands should be categorized (e.g., /status for status checks, /report for report generation, /settings for preferences). The maximum number of commands shown in the Telegram command menu is 100, but effective bots have fewer than 15 visible commands — more than 15 creates choice paralysis. For complex workflows that need more commands: use button-based navigation rather than text commands for secondary options; reserve slash commands for the most frequently used operations. The bot description and command descriptions (set via BotFather) are visible to users before they interact — use this space for onboarding, not just 'bot commands'.

WhatsApp Business Platform has country-specific pricing that varies significantly: messages to US phone numbers have different rates than messages to European or Southeast Asian numbers. As of 2023, Meta shifted to per-conversation pricing (not per-message): utility conversations (service notifications) cost less than marketing conversations; user-initiated service conversations within the 24-hour window are free. This pricing model penalizes bots that send many short messages in a single session (each message extends the conversation window but doesn't add cost within it) and makes long conversational threads economical. For cost management: batch multiple updates into a single message where possible; track conversation start times to understand when you're starting new (paid) conversations vs. continuing free ones; monitor marketing vs. utility conversation ratio — an unexpected shift toward marketing conversations signals a classification change that could increase costs.

Bot health monitoring requires tracking metrics that distinguish between platform API health and bot logic health, because both can fail independently. Platform-level metrics: webhook delivery success rate (the platform should deliver webhooks; if < 99.9%, there's a connectivity issue); API call success rate (your calls to the platform API; if < 99.5%, there's an auth or rate limit issue); average API latency. Bot logic metrics: message processing time (how long from webhook receipt to response sent); intent classification confidence distribution (sudden drop in high-confidence intents signals a model or prompt issue); fallback rate (the % of messages that hit the fallback handler). The most useful alert combination: high fallback rate + unchanged message volume = the bot is breaking on new input patterns; low message volume = users have stopped using the bot (channel death); high processing time = downstream service degradation.

Implementing end-to-end encrypted messaging for sensitive data in bot integrations requires: (1) generate an RSA-4096 or ECDH key pair per user at registration; (2) store the public key in your backend; store the private key encrypted with a user-derived key in the client; (3) for messages containing sensitive data (PII, credentials, confidential business info): encrypt the message content with the recipient's public key before sending to the messaging platform; (4) the messaging platform only sees ciphertext; (5) the recipient's client decrypts with their private key. This architecture ensures that even a compromised messaging platform account or a malicious insider at the platform cannot read sensitive message content. For bots that handle sensitive operations (password resets, financial authorizations, medical information): this is the only architecture that provides genuine privacy. Note: most messaging platform bots cannot implement E2E encryption through the standard API — this requires a custom client app.

Do not use messaging platform file sharing (Slack file upload, Telegram document messages) as a permanent file storage mechanism, because platforms enforce retention policies and file expiry. Slack deletes files according to the workspace admin's retention settings; Telegram bot-uploaded files expire after a period or when the bot's file cache is cleared. Any file shared via bot that is later referenced by a persistent link (URL, file_id) will 404 when the retention window passes. The correct file lifecycle: bots that receive files should immediately copy the file to permanent storage (S3, GCS) and store the permanent URL; bots that send files should generate a fresh upload from the permanent source at send time, not cache the platform's file_id for future resends. The failure is particularly insidious because it works perfectly during testing (files haven't expired yet) and fails silently in production months later.

Telegram group bot behavior requires explicit group permission management via the bot's Telegram settings. By default, bots in groups only receive messages that mention them (@bot) or commands. To receive all messages in a group, the bot must have the 'Group Privacy Mode' disabled in BotFather settings. The security implication: a bot with privacy mode disabled reads all group messages, which is an elevated access level that should be justified. The practical guidance: enable full message access only if the bot's function requires it (moderation bots, summarization bots); keep privacy mode enabled for bots that only need to respond to explicit commands.
